lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Intel Architecture (IA/x86) is the dominant computing architecture for PCs, servers, and cloud infrastructure. Continuously evolved through multiple generations.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
notes: IA32 and IA64 instruction sets; backbone of industry-standard computing covered in white paper.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Intel Xeon remains the dominant server processor family. Xeon Scalable (Sapphire Rapids, Emerald Rapids) are current. Some older generations entering EOL (Sapphire Rapids entered discontinuation Sept 2025) but platform is active.', 'source': 'https://wccftech.com/intel-announces-discontinuation-period-for-12th-generation-alder-lake-and-sapphire-rapids-scalable-cpus/'}
notes: Xeon MP widely used in 4-32 way mission-critical servers; E7501/E7505 chipsets.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Intel Itanium (IA-64) architecture fully discontinued. Final shipments July 2021, HPE support ended December 31, 2025. Architecture is dead.', 'source': 'https://www.tomshardware.com/news/intel-kills-itanium-processors-chips-hpe,38540.html'}
notes: Madison Itanium 2 released mid-2003; E8870 chipset; winning benchmark tests vs. RISC/Unix.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Standard-current. PCI Express Gen 5 widely deployed; Gen 6 specification released. Dominant I/O interconnect for servers and desktops.', 'source': 'https://pcisig.com/pci-express-60-specification'}
notes: Serial I/O replacing parallel PCI-X; 2.5 GHz per lane; up to x32; 10+ year architectural longevity predicted.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'PCI-X was superseded by PCI Express. No longer manufactured; discontinued from all modern server platforms.', 'source': 'General technology history'}
notes: PCI-X doubles PCI to 1.06 GB/s at 133 MHz; PCI-X 2.0 doubles again to 2.1 GB/s at 266 MHz; 4.2 GB/s at 533 MHz.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'DDR2 SDRAM succeeded by DDR3/4/5. Major manufacturers ending DDR3/DDR4 production by late 2025; DDR2 was already out of mainstream production years earlier.', 'source': 'https://www.thefpsreview.com/2025/02/18/leading-dram-manufacturers-will-phase-out-ddr3-and-ddr4-memory-this-year-its-claimed/'}
notes: Debuts 2004 as DDR2-400; draws half the power of DDR400; 40% less than DDR333; critical for blade/1U servers.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'DDR SDRAM (DDR1) was succeeded by DDR2, DDR3, DDR4, and DDR5. DDR1 has been out of mainstream production for over a decade. Fully discontinued by major manufacturers.', 'source': 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DDR_SDRAM'}
notes: DDR266 (2.1 GB/s), DDR333 (2.6 GB/s), DDR400 (3.2 GB/s per channel); dual-channel = 6.4 GB/s.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'IPMI (Intelligent Platform Management Interface) is legacy-supported. Succeeded by Redfish (DMTF standard) and modern BMC implementations. Still present in many existing servers but Redfish is the preferred modern standard.', 'source': 'https://www.dmtf.org/standards/redfish'}
notes: Hardware server management interface; 150+ adopters; v1.5 released 2001; enables heterogeneous server management, self-healing, predictive failure.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Dominant interconnect in HPC and AI data centers. InfiniBand switch sales surged Q2 2025 driven by NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra. NVIDIA continues manufacturing HDR/NDR InfiniBand products.', 'source': 'https://www.delloro.com/news/infiniband-switch-sales-surged-in-2q-2025-while-ethernet-maintains-market-lead-for-ai-back-end-networks/'}
notes: Low-latency data center communications; expected to blossom with PCI-E; 4x (10 Gbps) native IBA no longer bottlenecked by PCI-X.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Intel Xeon Nocona was a circa-2004 Xeon processor (first Prescott-based Xeon with EM64T). Long discontinued; succeeded through many subsequent Xeon generations.', 'source': 'https://ark.intel.com/content/www/us/en/ark/products/codename/1001/products-formerly-nocona.html'}
notes: Code name Nocona; debuts 2004 at higher clock speeds; complemented by Lindenhurst chipset.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Intel Lindenhurst was the E7520/E7320/E7525 chipset for Nocona Xeon (circa 2004). Long discontinued; succeeded by many subsequent Intel server chipset generations.', 'source': 'General Intel chipset history'}
notes: Industry's first chipset with PCI Express and DDR2; complements Nocona Xeon.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Intel Xeon MP Potomac was a circa-2004/2005 MP server processor. Long discontinued; succeeded by many subsequent Xeon MP/E7 generations.', 'source': 'General Intel processor history'}
notes: End-2004 32-bit server processor code name; 4-way supported by Twin Castle chipset.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Intel Twin Castle was the E8501 chipset for Xeon MP Potomac (circa 2004-2005). Long discontinued server chipset.', 'source': 'General Intel chipset history'}
notes: New 4-processor chipset for Potomac; Intel's first re-entrance into 4-way IA-32 since early 2002.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Intel 875P Canterwood chipset was introduced April 2003 as a high-end desktop/workstation chipset. Replaced by Intel 925X Alderwood in 2004. Long discontinued.', 'source': 'https://www.cnet.com/tech/tech-industry/intel-delays-new-pentium-4-chip/'}
notes: Q2-2003 high-end P4 chipset; ECC, Serial ATA, dual-channel DDR400 with PAT, GbE; Canterwood-ES adds PCI-X for true value servers.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Serial ATA (SATA) remains the dominant interface for consumer HDDs and SSDs, though NVMe (PCIe) is rapidly growing for performance SSDs. SATA is mature but widely active.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
notes: Serial disk connections for ATA drives used in mid-line storage; supported in Canterwood and future chipsets.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Gigabit Ethernet is mature and widely deployed. Now part of multi-gig standards (2.5G, 5G, 10GbE). Still the dominant LAN standard for endpoint connectivity.', 'source': 'General networking'}
notes: 20-30% throughput improvement with PCI-E; CPU utilization 10-20%; benefits clusters and iSCSI.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'iSCSI is actively used for IP-based SAN storage, especially in SMB and virtualized environments. Supported by VMware, Microsoft, and all major storage vendors.', 'source': 'General storage industry'}
notes: IP-based SCSI storage protocol benefiting from PCI-E x4/x8 bandwidth improvements.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'AGP (Accelerated Graphics Port) was phased out 2004-2010 in favor of PCI Express. Windows 10 v1607 (2016) dropped AGP support. By 2010 no new motherboard chipsets supported AGP. Fully discontinued.', 'source': 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accelerated_Graphics_Port'}
notes: Current 8x AGP graphics; to be superseded by PCI-E graphics with 2x throughput.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'ECC memory is actively used in servers, workstations, and increasingly in desktop systems (AMD Ryzen AM5 and Intel W680). ECC Memory market valued at $12.64B in 2024 with 6.6% CAGR.', 'source': 'https://www.maximizemarketresearch.com/market-report/ecc-memory-market/63727/'}
notes: Server-grade memory with single-bit error correction; future: memory mirroring, spare memory, memory scrubbing.

lifecycle_current: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'NewCard (also known as ExpressCard) was the PCMCIA replacement standard. ExpressCard failed to gain widespread use; manufacturers discontinued it as early as 2011. PCMCIA dissolved in 2010. Specification removed from website in 2018.', 'source': 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ExpressCard'}
notes: Standards-based replacement for PCMIA; smaller with less complex connectors; for mobile computers.
